summary: Nadia examines Arizona State University's radical reform under President Michael Crow, who rejected the Ivy League playbook of prestige-through-exclusivity in favor of a model measured by inclusion and student success. ASU reorganized into three pillars—Academic Enterprise (access and graduation), Knowledge Enterprise (use-inspired research), and Learning Enterprise (lifelong learning beyond degrees)—while climbing into the top 100 global research universities. Crow and Dabars argue against the 'Harvardization' of universities and the isomorphism that makes institutions converge on a generic model. Nadia connects ASU's pragmatic, entrepreneurial culture to tech values she admires, while arguing that tech still needs academia as civilization's primary infrastructure for knowledge production. She concludes that ASU demonstrates prestige need not correlate with exclusivity, and that longstanding institutions can reinvent themselves.
key_points: ASU rejected prestige-through-exclusivity, accepting 86% of applicants while climbing to top-100 research university status and improving graduation rates

Universities serve three incompatible functions—teaching, research, and career preparation—and ASU's reorganization into Academic, Knowledge, and Learning Enterprises makes these distinctions explicit
Tech and academia should be allies rather than adversaries, as academia remains civilization's primary professional sector dedicated to knowledge production
ASU's 'use-inspired research' philosophy prioritizes pragmatic, real-world impact over pure specialization, including consolidating academic departments to encourage interdisciplinary problem-solving
College alternatives like bootcamps serve a specific hiring gap but don't replace the broader benefits of a liberal arts education, especially for socioeconomically disadvantaged students
money_quotes: ASU is a comprehensive public research university, measured not by whom we exclude, but rather by whom we include and how they succeed.
Love it or hate it, academia is the closest we've ever come to having a multigenerational professional sector dedicated to the production of knowledge.

Academic social networks are some of the most refined and valuable structures our civilization has produced. Nowhere have we amassed a greater concentration of specialists focused full-time on knowledge production.
It's as if hungry customers were banging down the door of the French Laundry, whose owners throw up their hands and say 'Sorry, we don't have any reservations available!'
Here, the only mistake a dean can make, the biggest mistake, is not taking risks.
Prestige doesn't have to correlate to exclusivity; longstanding institutions are capable of reinventing themselves.
There's a reason why I prefer being in tech: everything moves faster, and people actually care about making ideas happen, not just talking about them.
They couldn't win the prestige game by competing directly against the Ivies, nor did they want to become a Generic Public University, so they made up a new status game.
key_insight: ASU demonstrates that universities can dramatically improve both research quality and educational access simultaneously by rejecting the exclusivity-as-prestige model and instead measuring success by whom they include and how those students succeed.
